content.text: The technologies behind AWS — on-demand compute, pay-per-use pricing, globally distributed storage — did not appear overnight. They are the result of six decades of incremental advances in computing, networking, and software architecture, each era solving the most pressing constraints of its time.
What It Is

The history of cloud computing is a progression from centralized mainframes shared by many users, through the decentralized personal computing era, back to centralized internet-based services — but this time with dramatically lower cost, dramatically higher scale, and programmable access for anyone with a credit card and a network connection.

Understanding this history is not mere trivia. The design choices embedded in AWS services — why S3 uses eventual consistency for certain operations, why EC2 availability zones exist, why IAM is structured around policies and roles — all trace back to real problems and failures encountered by specific organizations at specific points in this history. The history explains the architecture.
Why It Matters

Practitioners who understand only the present surface of cloud APIs make different — and often worse — architectural decisions than those who understand how those APIs came to be. Knowing that S3 was designed before consistent reads were guaranteed (eventual consistency was the original model until 2020) helps explain why certain distributed system patterns in AWS look the way they do. Knowing that EC2's original design was built on Xen explains why the Nitro transition mattered. Knowing that AWS grew from Amazon's internal infrastructure explains why it prioritized infrastructure services over application services initially.

History also provides the vocabulary for conversations about cloud strategy. Terms like "utility computing," "grid computing," "application service provider," "time-sharing," and "service-oriented architecture" all have specific technical meanings rooted in specific eras. Knowing them prevents misuse and enables clearer technical communication.
The Timeline

attrs.alt: Timeline of cloud computing history from 1960s mainframes to 2020s AI cloud

content.text: Era 1: Mainframes and Time-Sharing (1960s–1970s)

The first computer systems were enormous, expensive, and single-purpose. Organizations purchased or leased mainframes from IBM, and a team of operators managed the machine full-time. Early computing was batch-oriented: users submitted jobs (programs on punched cards) to an operator queue; the mainframe processed them sequentially; results were returned hours or days later. This model maximized machine utilization but provided zero interactivity.

Time-sharing changed this fundamentally. In 1962, MIT's Compatible Time-Sharing System (CTSS) allowed multiple users to interact with the computer simultaneously through teletype terminals. The operating system rapidly switched the CPU between user sessions, giving each user the illusion of a dedicated machine. This is the direct ancestor of the multi-tenancy model in modern cloud computing: one physical resource, many logical users, each isolated from the others.

IBM's VM/370 operating system (1972) took this further by running multiple complete operating system instances on a single mainframe — the first practical implementation of what we now call a hypervisor. Each user could run their own OS in a virtual machine, completely isolated from others. Every EC2 instance today is a direct descendant of this idea.

In 1969, ARPANET — the precursor to the internet — connected mainframes at four U.S. universities. John McCarthy, one of the founders of artificial intelligence, wrote in 1961 that computing might someday be organized as a public utility, with users paying for computing time as they paid for electricity. This vision — utility computing — would not be commercially realized for another 45 years.
Era 2: Minicomputers, Unix, and the PC (1970s–1980s)

The 1970s brought minicomputers — smaller, cheaper machines from DEC, HP, and others — that put computing within reach of individual departments rather than just whole organizations. Unix, developed at Bell Labs in 1969, introduced a portable, multi-user operating system that would become the foundation of nearly all server software, including the Linux kernel that runs virtually every cloud workload today.

The 1980s brought personal computers (Apple II, IBM PC) and the complete reversal of the centralized computing model. Instead of sharing remote mainframes, individuals owned their computing. This was excellent for individual productivity but created new problems: data silos, software incompatibility, no reliable way to collaborate, and every organization managing its own hardware.

Client-server architecture emerged as a compromise: servers centralized data and services; clients (desktop PCs) handled presentation and local processing. This model drove the enterprise IT industry through the 1990s and is the direct predecessor of cloud computing — the "server" in client-server just moved to a data center accessed over the internet.
Era 3: The Internet and Application Service Providers (1990s)

Tim Berners-Lee published the first web page in 1991. The commercial internet emerged in 1993–1995, and with it a new computing model: instead of running software locally, organizations could access software and data through a web browser. Application Service Providers (ASPs) appeared — companies that hosted software applications in their data centers and rented access to customers over the internet.

The ASP model foreshadowed SaaS but had significant limitations. ASPs typically hosted a single application per server (or per physical machine), used proprietary protocols, lacked automation, and required long contracts. Scaling required purchasing and provisioning additional physical hardware — a process measured in weeks or months. The ASP model was the right idea delivered with the wrong technology.

Grid computing also emerged in the 1990s as an academic and scientific approach to distributed computing: link thousands of computers across universities and research institutions to solve problems too large for any single machine. Projects like SETI@home (1999) used idle desktop CPUs worldwide to analyze radio telescope data. Grid computing demonstrated massive distributed computing at low cost but required highly specialized software and offered no commercial self-service provisioning.
Era 4: SaaS and the Dot-Com Era (1999–2005)

Salesforce, founded in 1999, made a radical claim for its era: enterprise software should be delivered as a service over the internet (Software as a Service), not installed on customer servers. "No Software" was its marketing slogan — provocative in a world where Oracle and SAP required enormous on-premises installations. Salesforce proved the model worked at enterprise scale and popularized the SaaS delivery pattern.

The dot-com boom (1995–2000) created enormous demand for internet infrastructure. Web companies built out massive data centers to handle anticipated growth, often wildly over-provisioning. When the dot-com crash came in 2000–2001, enormous quantities of server capacity sat idle. This over-built infrastructure became the physical foundation on which companies like Amazon offered compute resources for rent.

Amazon itself was building massive, highly automated infrastructure to run its own e-commerce platform. By the early 2000s, Amazon had developed internal systems for rapid, automated provisioning of compute and storage — systems that proved so useful that the company began to consider offering access to them as a commercial service.
Era 5: The Birth of AWS and Public IaaS (2002–2008)

Amazon Web Services as a brand appeared in 2002, initially offering a set of web APIs for integrating with Amazon's e-commerce platform. The pivotal moment came in 2003–2004 when Amazon executives, including Andy Jassy and Jeff Bezos, recognized that Amazon's internal infrastructure — its ability to deploy services rapidly on standardized, automated compute — was itself a valuable product. A memo circulated internally proposing to offer compute and storage as primitives to external developers.

Amazon launched Amazon Simple Storage Service (S3) in March 2006 — the first commercially available, pay-per-use, infinitely scalable object storage. The design philosophy was radical for its time: no capacity to provision, no infrastructure to manage, pay only for what you store and transfer, access via a simple HTTP API. S3 was followed in August 2006 by Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2), which allowed customers to rent virtual machines by the hour through an API, then terminate them when done.

EC2 in 2006 offered a single instance type (now called m1.small) at $0.10 per hour, running on Xen hypervisors. There were no Availability Zones, no VPC, no IAM, no Auto Scaling, and no SLA. But the core concept — rent a virtual machine by the hour, pay only for what you use — immediately attracted developers and startups who could now launch internet services without any upfront hardware investment.
The original EC2 API call (2006)
The earliest EC2 API used SOAP/XML over HTTP. Here is what launching an instance looked like in 2006 — before SDKs, before the CLI, and before IAM roles:

# This is historical pseudocode showing the original 2006 EC2 SOAP API structure. # Do NOT use this — it represents a deprecated interface. # Modern equivalent is shown in the Examples section below. # Original 2006 approach: SOAP XML over HTTP with query string signing # https://ec2.amazonaws.com/?Action=RunInstances # &ImageId=ami-60a54009 # The only public AMI available at launch # &MinCount=1 # &MaxCount=1 # &InstanceType=m1.small # The only instance type (0.1 vCPU, 1.7 GB RAM) # &AWSAccessKeyId=AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE # &Signature=...HMAC-SHA1 signature... # &Timestamp=2006-08-25T12:00:00Z # The response was XML: # # r-47a5402e # # # i-2ba64342 # ami-60a54009 # 0pending # m1.small # # #

By 2008, AWS had expanded to include Simple Queue Service (SQS), SimpleDB, and CloudFront. The same year, AWS added Availability Zones — a direct response to customers reporting that all their EC2 instances were on the same physical infrastructure and a single failure could take down their entire deployment. AZs remain one of the most important resilience features of AWS today.
Era 6: Cloud Goes Mainstream (2008–2014)

Google App Engine launched in 2008, offering a PaaS model: developers deployed Python applications without managing servers, and Google handled all infrastructure. App Engine introduced auto-scaling (the platform scaled capacity automatically) but imposed constraints — apps had to use Google's datastore and could not use arbitrary system calls. This trade-off between convenience and control would define the IaaS vs. PaaS debate for years.

Microsoft Azure launched in 2010, IBM SoftLayer (now IBM Cloud) followed, and the enterprise cloud market took shape. AWS added a series of foundational services during this period that remain central today: Elastic Load Balancing and Auto Scaling (2009), Amazon VPC (2009, generally available 2011), Amazon RDS (2009), Amazon DynamoDB (2012), and Amazon Glacier (2012). Each addressed a specific gap: VPC gave customers network isolation that shared EC2 lacked; RDS eliminated the operational burden of managing relational database servers; DynamoDB provided internet-scale NoSQL storage.

The Netflix migration to AWS, which began in 2008 and completed in 2015, became the defining case study for cloud adoption at scale. Netflix built the Simian Army (including Chaos Monkey) to deliberately inject failures into their AWS infrastructure, proving that a well-designed cloud architecture could survive the failures that make on-premises systems fragile. Their open-sourced tools and public engineering blog posts significantly influenced how the industry approached cloud reliability.
Era 7: Serverless, Containers, and the Modern Cloud (2014–Present)

AWS Lambda, launched in November 2014, introduced serverless computing: upload a function, AWS runs it in response to events, and billing is per invocation and per 100ms of execution time. With Lambda, the provisioning unit dropped from virtual machines (minutes to start) to function invocations (milliseconds). Lambda used Firecracker microVMs (open-sourced in 2018) internally to achieve both strong isolation and fast startup.

Docker launched in 2013 and containers became the dominant unit of application packaging by 2015. AWS responded with EC2 Container Service (ECS, 2014) and later Amazon EKS (2018, managed Kubernetes). Containers sit between VMs and serverless — faster than VMs to start, more flexible than Lambda, but requiring more operational management.

AWS Graviton processors (2018, Graviton2 in 2020, Graviton3 in 2021) marked AWS's entry into custom silicon. By designing its own ARM-based CPUs optimized for cloud workloads, AWS delivered 40% better price-performance than equivalent x86 instances for many workloads. This vertical integration — owning the hardware, the hypervisor, the network switches (Nitro Cards), and the software — is what allows AWS to compete on price while maintaining margins.

The 2020s brought AI and machine learning services to the forefront. AWS SageMaker (2017) democratized ML model training and deployment; Bedrock (2023) gave access to foundation models including Claude via API; Trainium and Inferentia custom chips optimized the cost of training and running large models. Cloud computing is now the primary substrate for the AI industry.
Benefits of Understanding This History
Explains service design decisions.

S3's strong read-after-write consistency for new objects but eventual consistency for overwrites (prior to December 2020) reflected distributed systems trade-offs that were necessary at S3's 2006 scale. Knowing this prevents misdesigning applications that assume consistency properties the service didn't originally guarantee.
Prevents repeating historical mistakes.

The patterns that failed at scale in the ASP era (single points of failure, manual scaling, opaque pricing) are the same patterns that create problems in cloud architectures today when applied naively.
Informs future direction.

The direction of cloud history — from coarse-grained (servers) to fine-grained (functions), from long-lived to ephemeral, from manual to fully managed — suggests where new services will appear and what trade-offs they will make.
Clarifies terminology.
"Cloud" is used loosely in industry conversation. History anchors the specific technical meanings of SaaS, PaaS, IaaS, serverless, and edge computing, enabling precise technical communication.
Trade-offs: What Each Era Got Wrong
Era
What it solved
What it left unsolved
Mainframe time-sharing
Hardware utilization, multi-user access
Cost, geographic reach, self-service
Personal computing
Individual access, cost, local control
Collaboration, data sharing, reliability
ASPs (1990s)
Outsourced software operation
Elasticity, API access, multi-tenancy efficiency
Early AWS IaaS (2006)
Self-service, elasticity, pay-per-use VMs
Network isolation, managed databases, containers
Serverless (2014+)
Zero infrastructure management, per-use billing
Cold start latency, long-running processes, debugging
How AWS Evolved: Key Service Milestones
The following timeline shows how AWS's service portfolio grew from a handful of storage and compute primitives to a 200+ service platform:

2002  AWS.com web services launch (e-commerce APIs)
2006  S3 (March), EC2 (August) — the founding services
2007  SimpleDB, Amazon DevPay
2008  CloudFront CDN, Elastic Block Store (EBS), Availability Zones
2009  SQS becomes generally available, Auto Scaling, ELB, Amazon VPC, RDS
2010  Amazon Route 53 (DNS), IAM (Identity and Access Management)
2011  Elastic Beanstalk (PaaS), CloudFormation (Infrastructure as Code)
2012  DynamoDB (NoSQL), Glacier (archival storage), Redshift (data warehouse)
2013  Amazon WorkSpaces (VDI), CloudTrail (audit logging)
2014  Lambda (serverless), ECS (containers), Aurora (MySQL-compatible managed DB)
2015  AWS Certificate Manager, AWS CodePipeline, Amazon API Gateway
2016  Amazon Rekognition (image AI), AWS Batch, Application Load Balancer
2017  SageMaker (ML platform), AWS Fargate (serverless containers)
2018  EKS (managed Kubernetes), Firecracker open-sourced, AWS Graviton (ARM)
2019  AWS Outposts (on-premises cloud), EventBridge
2020  AWS Graviton2 (40% better price/perf), S3 strong consistency, AWS Proton
2021  Graviton3, Amazon MemoryDB, AWS Private 5G
2022  AWS Supply Chain, Amazon Security Lake
2023  Amazon Bedrock (foundation models), Amazon CodeWhisperer GA
2024  Nova foundation models, expanded Graviton4 instances
When to Use Each Historical Model
Each era's computing model remains relevant for specific use cases today. The cloud did not render all previous models obsolete — it changed the default:
Batch computing (mainframe era).
AWS Batch revives this model for HPC and scientific workloads — submit jobs, the system runs them when resources are available. Appropriate for genomics, financial risk calculations, video rendering.
Dedicated servers (pre-cloud era).
EC2 Dedicated Hosts allocate an entire physical server to one customer — appropriate for licensed software tied to physical cores, or workloads with regulatory requirements against multi-tenancy.
Shared platform (ASP/PaaS era).
Elastic Beanstalk, App Runner, and Amplify offer managed deployment platforms where you provide code and the platform handles infrastructure — the modern, automated equivalent of ASPs.
On-premises hybrid (pre-internet era).
AWS Outposts brings AWS services to on-premises data centers — appropriate when latency or data residency requirements make fully cloud-hosted solutions impractical.
How to Implement: Tracing the Evolution via AWS APIs

The evolution from early to modern AWS is visible in the APIs themselves. The original EC2 API used Query String authentication (Signature Version 2); modern AWS uses Signature Version 4 with IAM roles. The following shows the modern approach to the same operations the 2006 API supported:

import boto3

# Modern equivalent of the 2006 EC2 RunInstances call
# Uses IAM roles (not hardcoded keys), Signature V4 (not V1),
# and supports hundreds of parameters that didn't exist in 2006

ec2 = boto3.client('ec2', region_name='us-east-1')

response = ec2.run_instances(
    # 2006: one AMI (ami-60a54009), one instance type (m1.small)
    # 2024: thousands of AMIs, 600+ instance types
    ImageId='ami-0abcdef1234567890',  # Amazon Linux 2023
    InstanceType='t3.micro',           # 2 vCPU, 1 GB RAM (2019, Nitro-based)
    MinCount=1,
    MaxCount=1,
    # These didn't exist in 2006:
    IamInstanceProfile={'Name': 'my-instance-role'},  # IAM roles (2010)
    NetworkInterfaces=[{                               # VPC (2009)
        'SubnetId': 'subnet-12345678',
        'DeviceIndex': 0,
        'AssociatePublicIpAddress': True,
        'Groups': ['sg-12345678']
    }],
    MetadataOptions={                                  # IMDSv2 (2019)
        'HttpTokens': 'required',
        'HttpEndpoint': 'enabled'
    },
    TagSpecifications=[{                               # Tagging (2010)
        'ResourceType': 'instance',
        'Tags': [{'Key': 'Name', 'Value': 'modern-ec2-demo'}]
    }]
)

instance_id = response['Instances'][0]['InstanceId']
print(f'Launched: {instance_id}')

import boto3

# The 2006 S3 API was REST/HTTP; the modern boto3 call is cleaner but
# the underlying model (bucket + key + value) is unchanged from 2006
s3 = boto3.client('s3')

# Create a bucket (same concept as 2006, now with more options)
s3.create_bucket(
    Bucket='my-demo-bucket-2024',
    CreateBucketConfiguration={'LocationConstraint': 'us-west-2'}
)

# Upload — same PUT semantics as 2006
s3.put_object(
    Bucket='my-demo-bucket-2024',
    Key='history/note.txt',
    Body=b'AWS launched S3 on March 14, 2006.',
    ServerSideEncryption='AES256'  # Encryption at rest (added 2011)
)

# The original 2006 S3 API returned eventual consistency for LIST operations
# After December 2020, S3 provides strong read-after-write consistency
# for all operations - this was a major reliability improvement
response = s3.get_object(Bucket='my-demo-bucket-2024', Key='history/note.txt')
print(response['Body'].read().decode())
Real-World Example: The Netflix Migration

Netflix began migrating from its own data centers to AWS in 2008, completing the migration in 2015. The trigger was a major database corruption incident in 2008 that caused a three-day service outage. Netflix concluded that building a fault-tolerant distributed system on AWS was more achievable than building equally resilient infrastructure in their own data centers.

The migration was a seven-year, complete rearchitecting of Netflix's platform — not a lift-and-shift. Netflix decomposed its monolithic application into hundreds of microservices, moved from relational databases to Cassandra and DynamoDB for most data, and built the global CDN (Open Connect) that delivers video. At the time of full migration in 2015, Netflix had grown from 1 million to 60 million subscribers and was streaming in 190 countries.

Netflix contributed the "Chaos Engineering" discipline to the industry: deliberately injecting failures (terminating random instances, blocking network paths, injecting latency) to verify that the system remained available. This was possible only because AWS provided rapid instance replacement — within seconds of an instance termination, Auto Scaling could launch a replacement. On-premises infrastructure made this kind of testing prohibitively slow and expensive.
Common Mistakes
Treating cloud migration as purely a cost story

Organizations that justify cloud migration purely on cost reduction frequently discover that a naive lift-and-shift does not reduce costs and may increase them. The cost benefits of cloud materialize through architectural changes: replacing idle provisioned capacity with elastic capacity, replacing self-managed software with managed services, automating provisioning that was previously manual. The history of every successful large-scale cloud migration is a history of rearchitecting, not just relocating.
Ignoring AWS's service deprecation patterns

AWS rarely removes services but does sunset features and add successors. SimpleDB (2007) was AWS's first managed database; it has never been shut down but is no longer recommended. EC2 Classic (pre-VPC) was retired in August 2022. Building on very early-generation services without checking whether a more capable successor exists leads to architectural debt.
Applying ASP-era thinking to cloud

ASPs hosted software on long-term contracts with manual scaling and no programmatic API. Cloud is fundamentally different: resources are API-provisioned, billed by usage, and expected to fail. Teams that provision "permanent" cloud servers without automation, treat cloud resources like physical servers with long operational lifetimes, and make no provision for automated failure recovery are reproducing ASP-era patterns in a cloud environment — losing the benefits of both models.
Best Practices
Learn from AWS service release notes.

The AWS "What's New" page records every service launch and major feature. Reading it periodically provides context for why services exist and what problems they solve. The history is still being written.
Prefer recent-generation services over legacy equivalents.

When multiple services solve the same problem, the later one typically incorporates lessons from the earlier one's limitations. Aurora is better than RDS for MySQL at scale; DynamoDB has evolved significantly since its 2012 launch (adding transactions, global tables, and on-demand billing).
Study the failure post-mortems.

AWS publishes post-mortems for significant service events. Each one describes a failure in the infrastructure, its cause, and the systemic changes made to prevent recurrence. These are the most instructive documents for understanding how distributed systems fail at cloud scale.
Understand the Availability Zone origin story.

AZs were added in 2008 because early customers running single-AZ deployments experienced correlated failures. Designing across AZs is not optional for production workloads — it exists specifically because single-point failures are a historical pattern.
Performance and Scalability: How the Bar Has Moved

The raw performance of EC2 instances has improved dramatically since 2006. The original m1.small offered 1 ECU (EC2 Compute Unit) of CPU, 1.7 GB of RAM, and 160 GB of instance storage with no guaranteed network bandwidth. A 2024 c7g.16xlarge (Graviton3) offers 64 vCPUs, 128 GB of RAM, 30 Gbps guaranteed network bandwidth, and 40 Gbps of EBS bandwidth — improvements of roughly 100× in CPU throughput, 75× in RAM, and several orders of magnitude in network performance.

Storage performance shows an even larger gap. The original EC2 instance store offered sequential read speeds in the hundreds of MB/s range. Modern i4i instances with NVMe SSDs deliver over 15 GB/s of sequential read throughput per instance. S3 throughput has scaled from megabytes to terabytes per second for a single bucket by automatically distributing data across thousands of storage nodes.

These improvements have compounded: the same dollar spent on EC2 today buys roughly 5–10× the compute it bought in 2010, driven by improvements in CPU architecture, hypervisor efficiency (Nitro vs. Xen), and storage hardware (NVMe SSDs vs. SATA HDDs). Understanding this trajectory helps predict where the next performance improvements will appear.
Tooling and Ecosystem Evolution
2006–2010: Raw API era.

Tools like elasticfox (a Firefox plugin for managing EC2), early Python libraries (boto, the predecessor of boto3), and hand-rolled SOAP/REST clients. Managing AWS meant writing code against low-level HTTP APIs.
2011–2014: Infrastructure as code emerges.
CloudFormation (2011) introduced declarative infrastructure templates. Chef and Puppet extended into cloud configuration management. Vagrant (2010) standardized development environments.
2014–2018: Container tooling explodes.

Docker, Docker Compose, Kubernetes, Helm, ECS, and EKS transformed how applications are packaged and deployed. Terraform (2014) offered provider-agnostic IaC that became the dominant multi-cloud tool.
2019–present: Developer experience and AI tooling.

AWS CDK (2019) let developers define cloud infrastructure using TypeScript, Python, and other programming languages. AWS CodeWhisperer and Amazon Q provide AI-assisted coding and cloud operations. The tooling layer is now as important as the infrastructure layer itself.
Security and Reliability: How Guarantees Evolved

Early AWS had no SLA for EC2 (2006), no IAM (access was controlled only by account-level API keys), no VPC (all instances ran on a shared flat network), and no CloudTrail (no audit logging of API calls). Each of these gaps was discovered through real customer incidents.

IAM (2010) was introduced after customers struggled to manage access for teams — every employee sharing a single API key pair was an obvious security and operational risk. VPC (2009, GA 2011) addressed the complaint that EC2 Classic exposed all instances to the public internet by default. CloudTrail (2013) came after multiple incidents where customers could not determine who had made changes to their infrastructure. The current security posture of AWS is the accumulated response to a decade of real-world security challenges.

The same evolution applies to SLAs. AWS now offers 99.99% availability SLAs for Multi-AZ RDS and EC2 running across multiple AZs — guarantees that simply could not have been made in 2006 when the infrastructure was less mature and the failure mode catalog was unknown. The current SLAs represent contractual confidence built on operational experience.
Mental Model

Think of cloud computing's history as a series of problems and solutions, each solution revealing the next problem. Mainframes solved single-user limitations → revealed cost and access barriers. Personal computers solved cost → revealed collaboration and reliability barriers. The internet and ASPs solved collaboration → revealed scalability and automation barriers. AWS solved automation and scalability → revealed security, networking, and managed service gaps. Serverless solved operational overhead → revealed cold start and debugging gaps. Each generation builds on the previous while solving its most painful limitations.

This pattern also predicts the future: the current gap being actively worked on is the boundary between fully managed services and the expertise required to use them correctly. AI-assisted cloud operations (Amazon Q, AWS CodeWhisperer) are the current attempt to solve the problem of cloud complexity — the same problem that IAM, CloudFormation, and CDK partially addressed before it.
Comparison with Alternative Historical Paths
Model
Peak era
Still used?
Modern AWS equivalent
Time-sharing
Yes (cloud IS time-sharing)
EC2, Lambda
Grid computing
Niche (research)
AWS Batch, ParallelCluster
Colocation (colo)
1990s–present
Yes (physical hardware)
AWS Direct Connect + on-prem
Utility computing
Concept from 1961
Yes — this IS cloud
All AWS services
Key Takeaways

Cloud computing is the commercial realization of John McCarthy's 1961 vision of computing as a utility — pay per use, shared infrastructure, self-service access. The concept is 60 years old; the commercial implementation is less than 20.

AWS launched S3 in March 2006 and EC2 in August 2006, establishing the IaaS model that Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud Platform would follow. These two services remain foundational to every AWS architecture today.

Each major AWS service addition — VPC (2009), IAM (2010), Lambda (2014), EKS (2018) — was a direct response to a gap identified through real customer failures and requests. The service catalog is a map of discovered infrastructure needs.

The history of cloud follows a consistent arc: each era solves the most painful constraint of the previous era, revealing the next constraint. Mainframes → minicomputers → PCs → internet → cloud → serverless is a sequence driven by cost and friction reduction.

Netflix's 2008–2015 migration remains the canonical large-scale cloud adoption case study — showing that successful cloud migration requires architectural transformation, not just infrastructure relocation.

Performance has compounded dramatically: the same dollar spent on EC2 today buys roughly 5–10× the compute it bought in 2010, driven by custom silicon (Graviton), hypervisor advances (Nitro), and hardware improvements.

AWS's current trajectory — AI services, custom silicon, edge computing, and developer tooling — follows the same historical pattern: addressing the next-most-painful constraint, which is now the expertise gap required to use the cloud correctly.
